signals.description: Problem asks for minimum or maximum over a set of independent, irreversible choices where local optimality extends to global optimality.

paradigms.rationale: Independent irreversible choices with exchange-argument provability are the hallmark of greedy.

structures.rationale: Priority queue maintains the best available choice at each step (Dijkstra, Prim).
Sort-then-scan greedy often reduces to a two-pointer sweep over sorted intervals.

strategies.rationale: Two-pass greedy (left-to-right then right-to-left) handles bidirectional constraints.
Sorting by a key transforms the problem into a sequential scan.

signals.description: Problem involves counting, minimizing, or maximizing over an exponential decision space where subproblems recur.

paradigms.rationale: Overlapping subproblems with optimal substructure is the defining DP signal.
When state space is small, backtracking with memoization converges to DP.

structures.rationale: Memo table (hash map) stores computed subproblem results for top-down DP.
1D rolling array optimizes space when current row depends only on previous.

strategies.rationale: Push DP (update successors) vs pull DP (collect from predecessors) yields structurally different solutions.
Suffix DP vs prefix DP can simplify transitions or base cases.

signals.description: Problem asks for the shortest, cheapest, or fewest-step path between two states in a state space.

paradigms.rationale: Shortest path is the canonical graph traversal problem.
On DAGs, shortest path reduces to DP in topological order.

structures.rationale: The state space must be encoded as a graph (explicit or implicit).
Dijkstra requires a priority queue for weighted shortest path.

strategies.rationale: Bidirectional BFS splits the search from both ends, reducing branching factor.
Implicit graph construction transforms the problem into explicit BFS/Dijkstra.

signals.description: Problem asks to generate, enumerate, or count all valid configurations satisfying constraints.

paradigms.rationale: Exhaustive enumeration with pruning is the core backtracking use case.
When only counting (not listing), DP avoids redundant enumeration.

structures.rationale: HashSet tracks visited states to avoid duplicate exploration.
Bitmask represents subset state compactly for small universes (N <= 20).

strategies.rationale: Counting valid = total minus invalid, when invalid is simpler to count.
Meet-in-the-middle splits enumeration into two halves and combines.

signals.description: Data arrives as a stream of unknown or very large length and must be processed with bounded memory.

paradigms.rationale: Reservoir sampling and sketches handle streams with bounded memory.
Event-driven processing naturally models stream consumption.

structures.rationale: Two-heap partition maintains running median in a stream.
Count-Min Sketch and frequency maps handle stream frequency queries.

strategies.rationale: Stream forces online processing; choosing online vs offline is the key strategic decision.
Lazy deletion in heaps avoids expensive restructuring during stream processing.

signals.description: Problem involves divisibility, prime factors, GCD/LCM, or modular arithmetic.

paradigms.rationale: Number theory primitives (sieve, GCD, modular exponentiation) directly apply.

structures.rationale: Counting arrays store factor frequencies or sieve results.
Bitset sieve is a space-efficient prime sieve implementation.

strategies.rationale: Reducing to modular arithmetic often transforms exponential to logarithmic.
Inclusion-exclusion over prime factors counts coprime integers efficiently.

signals.description: Problem involves tasks or items with prerequisite or dependency relationships that constrain ordering.

paradigms.rationale: Topological sort via DFS or Kahn's algorithm resolves dependency ordering.

structures.rationale: Dependencies are naturally modeled as directed edges in a DAG.
In-degree map tracks remaining prerequisites for Kahn's algorithm.

strategies.rationale: Push (BFS/Kahn) vs pull (DFS post-order) yields different topological sort implementations.
Reverse post-order DFS produces topological ordering.

signals.description: Problem asks whether elements are connected, how many components exist, or requires merging groups.

paradigms.rationale: BFS/DFS discovers connected components.

structures.rationale: Union-Find is purpose-built for dynamic connectivity queries.
Adjacency list or implicit grid represents the connectivity structure.

strategies.rationale: Offline edge processing allows reverse-time DSU for deletion problems.
Processing edge deletions in reverse converts them to additions, enabling DSU.

signals.description: For each element, find the next or previous element that is greater or smaller.

paradigms.rationale: Monotonic stack is a greedy eviction of dominated elements.
Segment tree can answer range min/max queries for similar problems.

structures.rationale: Monotonic stack computes next greater/smaller in O(n) by evicting dominated elements.

strategies.rationale: Right-to-left scan with monotonic stack computes next greater trivially.
Each element pushed and popped at most once gives O(n) amortized total.

signals.description: Problem asks for the longest, shortest, or optimal contiguous subarray satisfying a monotone property.

paradigms.rationale: Variable-size window greedily expands and shrinks.
Kadane's algorithm is DP on subarrays (maximum subarray sum).

structures.rationale: Sliding window is the canonical structure for contiguous subarray optimization.
Frequency map inside the window tracks character/element counts.

strategies.rationale: Left and right pointers each advance at most N times, yielding O(n) amortized.
'Exactly K distinct' = 'at most K' minus 'at most K-1' uses complement counting.

signals.description: Problem requires answering aggregate queries over ranges with intermixed point or range updates.

paradigms.rationale: Segment tree is built via divide-and-conquer over the array range.
Prefix sum handles static range queries in O(1) after O(n) build.

structures.rationale: Segment tree or Fenwick tree provides O(log n) range query + update.

strategies.rationale: Lazy propagation defers range updates to achieve O(log n) per range update.
Coordinate compression maps sparse value ranges to dense indices for tree structures.

signals.description: Problem asks to minimize a maximum or maximize a minimum, where feasibility is monotone in the answer.
keywords: is it possible in time T

paradigms.rationale: Binary search on the answer space is a divide-and-conquer reduction.
The feasibility check inside binary search is often a greedy scan.

structures.rationale: Feasibility check may use two-pointer to verify capacity constraints.

strategies.rationale: Transforms optimization into a decision problem, then binary searches over the answer.

signals.description: Problem describes an explicit step-by-step process with defined rules to execute.

paradigms.rationale: Direct simulation follows the described process rules.

structures.rationale: Hash map tracks state for cycle detection or memoization.
Event-driven simulation uses a priority queue to process events in time order.

strategies.rationale: Event-driven (lazy) simulation advances only at events vs tick-by-tick (eager).
Reverse simulation can convert deletions into additions.

signals.description: Problem involves finding a cycle in a sequence of states or linked structure.

paradigms.rationale: Floyd's cycle detection is a simulation with two pointers at different speeds.
Pigeonhole principle guarantees a cycle exists when values exceed range.

structures.rationale: Fast/slow pointer technique detects cycles in O(1) space.
HashSet of visited states provides simple cycle detection.

strategies.rationale: Floyd's algorithm runs in O(n) amortized by pointer advancement bounds.

signals.description: Problem involves prefix matching, autocomplete, or operations over strings sharing common prefixes.

paradigms.rationale: Trie structure decomposes strings by character position (divide by prefix).

structures.rationale: Trie provides O(L) prefix lookup and supports prefix enumeration.
Rolling hash enables substring matching without a trie.

strategies.rationale: Trie compresses the string dimension into a tree, reducing multi-string operations.
String matching can be transformed to automaton traversal.

signals.description: Problem involves subsets of a small universe (N <= 20) with set operations like union, intersection, or membership.

paradigms.rationale: Bitmask DP iterates over 2^N states with transitions that flip bits.
Branch and bound on subsets is viable for small N.

structures.rationale: Bitmask represents subset state in O(1) per set operation.

strategies.rationale: Meet-in-the-middle splits bitmask space into two halves for N up to 40.
Bitmask DP transitions can push (set bit) or pull (check predecessor masks).

signals.description: Problem involves searching or querying in sorted or nearly sorted data.

paradigms.rationale: Binary search halves the search space at each step.

structures.rationale: Two pointers on sorted data solve pair-sum and partition problems.
BST provides dynamic sorted data with O(log n) search.

strategies.rationale: Reducing optimization to a monotone predicate enables binary search.

signals.description: Problem asks to count combinations, permutations, or arrangements, often with large N requiring modular arithmetic.

paradigms.rationale: Combinatorial identities and modular arithmetic directly compute counts.
DP builds counting tables when closed-form identities are unavailable.

structures.rationale: Memo table caches computed binomial coefficients.

strategies.rationale: Reducing to known sequences (Catalan, Fibonacci) avoids direct enumeration.
Inclusion-exclusion counts valid arrangements via complement subtraction.

signals.description: Problem asks to find pairs or groups of elements satisfying a relationship (sum, difference, product).

paradigms.rationale: Complement lookup reduces pair-finding to arithmetic.

structures.rationale: Two-sum complement map provides O(1) partner lookup.
Two pointers on sorted data find pairs in O(n).

strategies.rationale: Transform 'find pair summing to K' into 'for each x, check if K-x exists'.
4-sum splits into pairs and uses hash map for cross-half complement.

signals.description: Problem involves intervals that may overlap and asks about scheduling, merging, or covering.

paradigms.rationale: Sort-then-scan greedy handles interval scheduling and merging.

structures.rationale: Min-heap tracks end times for meeting room allocation.
Segment tree handles interval stabbing queries with updates.

strategies.rationale: Sweep line reduces 2D interval problems to 1D by fixing one axis.
Converting intervals to events (start/end points) enables sweep processing.

signals.description: Problem has natural recursive decomposition where subproblems are independent and structurally identical.

paradigms.rationale: Independent, structurally identical subproblems define divide-and-conquer.

structures.rationale: Segment tree encodes the recursive decomposition as a balanced tree.

strategies.rationale: Bottom-up merge (pull) vs top-down distribution (push) defines the recursive flow.
Recursive geometric decomposition reduces dimensions at each level.

signals.description: Problem involves finding missing, duplicate, or unique elements using XOR cancellation or parity properties.

paradigms.rationale: XOR cancellation is an algebraic identity that eliminates paired elements.

structures.rationale: XOR operations are native bit manipulation.
HashSet provides an alternative for finding unique elements.

strategies.rationale: XOR of all elements cancels pairs, leaving the complement (unique element).
Transform 'find missing' into 'XOR all values with all indices'.

signals.description: Problem operates on a rooted or unrooted tree and asks about subtree properties, paths, or distances.

paradigms.rationale: Tree DP computes subtree properties via parent-child transitions.
DFS traversal is the foundation for tree algorithms.

structures.rationale: Tree stored as adjacency list for DFS-based processing.
Euler tour + segment tree supports path queries and subtree aggregation.
DSU on tree (small-to-large merging) handles subtree queries.

strategies.rationale: Pull (bottom-up subtree DP) then push (top-down rerooting) covers full-tree answers.

antiOverlapRules.description: Jaccard similarity on combined key sets must be below threshold.
antiOverlapRules.check: For two paths A and B, compute the union of their {paradigm, structure, strategy} ID sets. If |A intersect B| / |A union B| > overlapThreshold, the paths are considered overlapping and one should be replaced.

antiOverlapRules.description: Paradigm diversity: no two paths in a set of three may share the same primary paradigm.
antiOverlapRules.check: For each path, identify the paradigm with the highest weight. If two paths in the final selection share the same highest-weight paradigm ID, replace the lower-weighted one with a path using a different primary paradigm.

antiOverlapRules.description: Structure diversity: at least two distinct primary structures must appear across three paths.
antiOverlapRules.check: For each path, identify the structure with the highest weight. Count distinct structure IDs across all selected paths. If fewer than two distinct structures appear, substitute a path that introduces a different structure.

antiOverlapRules.description: Strategy diversity: at least two distinct primary strategies must appear across three paths.
antiOverlapRules.check: For each path, identify the strategy with the highest weight. Count distinct strategy IDs across all selected paths. If fewer than two distinct strategies appear, substitute a path that introduces a different strategy.

antiOverlapRules.description: No identical path triples: the ordered tuple (paradigm, structure, strategy) of primary keys must be unique per path.
antiOverlapRules.check: For each path, form the tuple (highest-weight paradigm ID, highest-weight structure ID, highest-weight strategy ID). If any two paths produce the same tuple, they are duplicates and one must be replaced with a path producing a different tuple.
